api.description:
Returns a paginated list of the account's invoices and debit notes (and advance payments when that feature is enabled), most recent first.

The list supports rich filtering and sorting through the query parameters below.

By default each item contains the invoice header and its client only. Pass `recursive=1` to also receive the nested line items, payments, taxes, documents and custom fields.

Results are paginated (20 per page by default).

Returns a single **standard invoice** by id, with full nested detail — line items, payments, taxes, documents, custom fields and the client — in one response (no `recursive` flag needed).

Only standard invoices (document type Invoice) are returned here; an id belonging to another document type (estimate, credit note, debit note, refund, …) returns **404 Invoice not found** — use that type's own single-resource endpoint instead.

Requires permission to view the invoice (all-invoices access, or being the invoice's own staff, plus invoice-details access).

staff_id.description: The staff member who created the invoice. `0` means the account owner.

subscription_id.description: The parent invoice this one follows (for example, the invoice being refunded). Leave null if the invoice has no parent.

store_id.description: The store / warehouse used for stock deduction. Optional — when omitted, the primary store is used. A store can also be set per line item via `InvoiceItem.store_id`.

no.description: Invoice number. Auto-generated when omitted, but can be overridden.

name.description: Used for templates and subscriptions only.

branch_id.description: The branch for this invoice. The branch must be active. Get the id from the General Listing API (Branch).

client_id.description: The client this invoice belongs to. **Required.** Get the id from the Clients API.

is_offline.description: Whether the client is offline (0 or 1).

currency_code.description: Currency code (for example, `USD`). Optional — defaults to the account currency.

client_email.description: Client's email. Required only when the client's invoicing method is email; otherwise it must be a valid email when provided.

date.description: Invoice date (YYYY-MM-DD). Must fall within an open financial period.

discount.description: Percentage discount, from 0 to 100. Mutually exclusive with `discount_amount` (set the other to 0). Cannot exceed the account/staff maximum discount.

discount_amount.description: Fixed discount amount. Mutually exclusive with `discount` (set the other to 0). Cannot exceed the account/staff maximum discount.

deposit_type.description:
Deposit type.

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1 | Unpaid |
| 2 | Paid |

`0` means not set.

notes.description: Notes shown to the client (up to 80000 characters).

invoice_layout_id.description: Layout used to render the invoice. The default layout is used when omitted.

estimate_id.description: The estimate this invoice was created from.

shipping_options.description:
Controls how shipping / client details are shown.

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| (empty) | Auto |
| 1 | Hide shipping options |
| 2 | Show main client details |
| 3 | Show secondary client details |

client_active_secondary_address.description: Whether the secondary address is active (0 or 1).

requisition_delivery_status.description:
Requisition delivery status.

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1 | Pending |
| 2 | Not All Available |
| 3 | Accepted |
| 4 | Cancelled |
| 5 | Modified |

pos_shift_id.description: POS session id. When set, the store is taken from the POS session.

terms.description: Terms and conditions text (up to 80000 characters).

date_format.description: Date format. Defaults to the account date format.

Invoice.description: Invoice header. `client_id` is the only required field; most other fields are optional and several are filled automatically when omitted (currency, language, date format, store).

item.description: Line label / name. Optional when creating via the API.

unit_price.description: Unit price. Enter the number only, with no currency symbol.

tax1.description: Primary tax id applied to the line.

tax2.description: Secondary (stacked) tax id applied to the line.

product_id.description: Product id. When branch management is enabled, the product must exist in the current branch.

discount.description: Line discount. Cannot exceed the maximum discount.

discount_type.description:
Discount type.

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1 | Percentage |
| 2 | Value |

store_id.description: Store / warehouse for this line's stock deduction.

lot.description: Lot / batch numbers for tracked products.

expiry_date.description: Expiry dates matching `lot`, for tracked products.

InvoiceItem.description: Invoice line items. Normally provided when creating an invoice.

payment_method.description: Payment method. **Required.** Must be one of your account's active payment methods.

treasury_id.description: Treasury that receives the payment. When omitted, a default treasury is chosen automatically.

date.description: Payment date. Defaults to today when omitted.

staff_id.description: Staff member who recorded the payment. Defaults to `0` (owner).

Payment.description: Optional payments recorded together with the invoice.

InvoiceCustomField.description: Custom field values defined for invoices.

api.description:
Creates multiple invoices in a single request. The request body is a **JSON array**; each element has the exact same shape as the body of **Add New Invoice** (an object with an `Invoice` member plus optional `InvoiceItem`, `Payment`, etc.).

Invoices are created **one by one and the operation is not atomic** — if one element fails validation the others are still created. The response is a **JSON array** with one result object per submitted invoice, in the same order as the request. A successful element carries the new invoice `id` and number; a failed element carries an error result with a message, so you must inspect each element to confirm it succeeded.

Per-element fields and defaulting behaviour are identical to **Add New Invoice** — see that endpoint for the full field reference.

source_id.description: The id of the source document referenced by `source_type`.

client_email.description: Client's email. Required only when the client's invoicing method is email; otherwise it must be a valid email when provided.

client_active_secondary_address.description: Whether the secondary address is active (0 or 1).

InvoiceItem.description: Invoice line items. Normally provided when creating an invoice.

item.description: Line label / name. Optional when creating via the API.

unit_price.description: Unit price. Enter the number only, with no currency symbol.

tax1.description: Primary tax id applied to the line.

tax2.description: Secondary (stacked) tax id applied to the line.

product_id.description: Product id. When branch management is enabled, the product must exist in the current branch.

discount.description: Line discount. Cannot exceed the maximum discount.

discount_type.description:
Discount type.

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1 | Percentage |
| 2 | Value |

store_id.description: Store / warehouse for this line's stock deduction.

lot.description: Lot / batch numbers for tracked products.

expiry_date.description: Expiry dates matching `lot`, for tracked products.

Payment.description: Optional payments recorded together with the invoice.

payment_method.description: Payment method. **Required.** Must be one of your account's active payment methods.

treasury_id.description: Treasury that receives the payment. When omitted, a default treasury is chosen automatically.

date.description: Payment date. Defaults to today when omitted.

staff_id.description: Staff member who recorded the payment. Defaults to `0` (owner).

InvoiceCustomField.description: Custom field values defined for invoices.

api.description:
Edit an existing **standard invoice** by id. Send via `PUT /invoices/{id}` (or `POST /invoices/{id}`) with the full invoice payload — submitted line items, payments and custom fields replace the existing ones.

The invoice **cannot be edited** (returns 400 or 404) when it: is on a different branch than it was created on; has a valid (accepted) electronic invoice; has related credit charges, installment agreements or membership/credit renewals; falls in a closed financial period (non-draft); is tied to a closed POS shift; has a received requisition; or is selected in a production plan.

Requires edit permission — all-invoices edit access, or being the invoice's own staff with edit-own access.

**Access:** requires permission to edit/remove invoice payments — either across all payments, or limited to the caller's own payments. Payments tied to an advance-payment invoice, and (when automatic invoice payment is enabled) client-credit payments, cannot be deleted via the API.

items.name: How To Authorize
items.content:
To access Daftra's API securely, every request must be authorized. Daftra supports two authorization methods to accommodate different use cases:

---

## Method 1: Using API Key 

This is the simplest way to authorize requests. You can generate and use an API key directly from your Daftra account.

:::info[]
When using API Key Authentication, make sure to unmark the "Authorization (access token)" header in your API request. Even with a correct API key, leaving it enabled will cause an authentication error.

![Authorization .png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/355145/image-preview)
:::

### Steps to Get Your API Key:

1. Log in to your Daftra account  
2. Navigate to: **Settings → API → API Key**  
3. Generate or copy your existing API Key  
4. Include it in the header of each request using the format:

> **Important:** Keep your API Key secure. Do not expose it in public repositories or client-side code.

---

## Method 2: Token-Based Authentication (OAuth 2.0)

This method is recommended for integrations requiring dynamic access, such as third-party applications connecting on behalf of users.

### How It Works:

1. Request an **access token** using your credentials or through an authorization grant  
2. Use the token in your API requests

items.name: Endpoints Structure
items.content:
This guide walks you through how to use a GET endpoint in Daftra’s API to retrieve data from specific entities such as products, clients, or invoices.

## Steps to Use the Endpoint:
1. Press on the Folder in the API panel.
1. Locate the endpoint: **/v2/api/entity/{entity}/list/1** 
1. Move your mouse cursor over the entity name to show the entities’ dropdown menu.
1. Click on the entity you want and it will be copied automatically.
1. On the right panel under Path Params, paste the copied value into the entity Parameter **“Value”** field .
1. Switch the environment to "v2".
1. Press **“Send”**.


**Note:** Make sure you are properly authenticated before sending the request., for more details refer to the [Authorization guide](https://docs.daftara.dev/933385m0).

items.name: API Improvements
items.content:
Welcome to Daftra API V2, the next-generation API designed for enhanced flexibility, precision, and control over your business data. This upgraded version introduces advanced filtering capabilities, multi-level data access, and optimized performance for modern integrations.

---

## What's New in V2

Discover the key improvements that make API V2 more powerful, scalable, and data-rich than ever before.

- **Enhanced Data Retrieval** for accessing richer, more detailed, and relational information across entities.
- **Advanced filtering system** with dynamic query capabilities
- **Multi-level data access** (up to 4 levels of related data)
- **Expanded data scope** with comprehensive record relationships
- **Better data structuring** to reduce API calls

---

## Base URL

Understand how to access the API and securely authenticate each request.

```
Base URL: https://your-domain.daftra.com/v2/api
```

:::note[]
For more details on how to authenticate your API requests, please refer to the [Authentication guide](https://docs.daftara.dev/933385m0).
:::

---

## Core Endpoints Structure

Learn how to list, fetch, and explore core entities using consistent URL patterns.

### List Entities

Retrieve lists of records with a specified level of detail:

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/{entity_name}/list/{level}
```

### Get Single Entity

Fetch a specific record by its ID with level-based detail:

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/{entity_name}/{id}/{level}
```

---

## Common Entities

Explore the most commonly used entities, their endpoints, and supported levels.

| Entity       | Entity Name        | Levels | Description                                  |
| ------------ | ------------------ | ------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| **Invoices** | `/invoice`         | 1-4    | Sales invoices with items, clients, payments |
| **Clients**  | `/client`          | 1-3    | Customer records with contacts, addresses    |
| **Products** | `/product`         | 1-2    | Product catalog with categories, pricing     |
| **Orders**   | `/sales_order`     | 1-4    | Sales orders with items and fulfillment      |
| **Payments** | `/invoice_payment` | 1-3    | Payment records with invoices, methods       |
| **Expenses** | `/expense`         | 1-3    | Expense tracking with categories, receipts   |
| **Quotes**   | `/estimate`        | 1-4    | Price quotes with items, conversions         |

## Multi-Level Data Access

Daftra V2 introduces a **4-level tiered data structure** allowing you to control the depth of related data in your responses.

### Level Structure

Understand what each level of data includes and when to use it.
| Level | Description | Use Case |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Level 1** | Basic entity data only | Quick listings, minimal data |
| **Level 2** | + Directly related data | Standard operations |
| **Level 3** | + Nested relationships | Detailed views |
| **Level 4** | + Deep context & connections | Full data analysis |

### Examples

```http
# Level 1: Basic invoice data
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1

# Level 2: Invoice + client info
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/2

# Level 3: Invoice + client + items + taxes
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/3

# Level 4: Invoice + all relationships + nested data
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/4
```

## Pagination & Sorting

Manage large datasets with page-based navigation and sorting options.

### Pagination Parameters

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/2?page=1
```

### Sorting

```http
# Single field
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1?sort[created_date]=desc
```

### Response with Pagination

```json
{
  "data": [...],
  "meta": {
    "current_page": 1,
    "total": 250,
    "last_page": 5,
    "from": 1,
    "to": 50
  },
  "links": {
    "first": "/.../list/2?page=1",
    "last": "/.../list/2?page=5",
    "prev": null,
    "next": "/.../list/2?page=2"
  }
}
```

---

## Performance Best Practices

### 1. **Choose Appropriate Levels**

- Use Level 1 for listings and quick data
- Use Level 3-4 only when you need the related data
- Higher levels = more processing time

### 2. **Pagination**

- Always use pagination for large datasets
- Use `total` count to determine if more pages exist

---

items.name: Filtering
items.content:
## Advanced Filtering

Learn how to refine your API requests using flexible filtering options. You can apply filters by value, range, status, and more.

### Basic Filters

Use a simple filter to retrieve records that match a single condition (e.g., unpaid invoices).

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1?filter[payment_status]=0
```

### Multiple Filters

Apply more than one filter in a single request to narrow down results based on multiple criteria.

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1?filter[payment_status]=0&filter[summary_total][gte]=1000
```

### Date Range Filters

Filter results within a specific date range using `gte` (greater than or equal) and `lte` (less than or equal) operators.

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/2?filter[date][gte]=2024-01-01&filter[date][lte]=2024-12-31
```

### OR Filters

OR logic without indexes when you don't need grouping. Use `filter[or]` without numeric indexes.

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/purchase_order_item/list/1?filter[or][tracking_data][like]=LOT001&filter[or][tracking_data][like]=LOT002
```


### Operator Support

| Operator    | Description                | Example                                                         |
| ----------- | -------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `eq`        | Equals (default)           | `filter[payment_status]=0`                                      |
| `not_equal` | Not equals                 | `filter[payment_status][not_equal]=0`                           |
| `gt`        | Greater than               | `filter[summary_total][gt]=1000`                                |
| `gte`       | Greater than or equal      | `filter[summary_total][gte]=1000`                               |
| `lt`        | Less than                  | `filter[summary_total][lt]=5000`                                |
| `lte`       | Less than or equal         | `filter[summary_total][lte]=5000`                               |
| `from`      | From value (alias for gte) | `filter[date][from]=2024-01-01`                                 |
| `to`        | To value (alias for lte)   | `filter[date][to]=2024-12-31`                                   |
| `like`      | Contains (text search)     | `filter[business_name][like]=john`                              |
| `in`        | In (array)                 | `filter[payment_status][in][]=0&filter[payment_status][in][]=1` |
| `not_in`    | Not in (array)             | `filter[available_online][not_in][]=1`                          |
| `is_null`   | Is null                    | `filter[deleted_at][is_null]=1`                                 |

### Advanced Examples

#### Combining Multiple Operators

Filter for invoices that are unpaid and have a total between 1000 and 5000:

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1?filter[payment_status]=0&filter[summary_total][gte]=1000&filter[summary_total][lte]=5000
```

#### Using IN Operator

Filter for multiple payment statuses:

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1?filter[payment_status][in][]=0&filter[payment_status][in][]=1&filter[payment_status][in][]=2
```

#### Text Search with LIKE

Find customers with names containing "john":

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/customer/list/1?filter[business_name][like]=john
```

#### Null Check

Find records where a field is null:

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/invoice/list/1?filter[notes][is_null]=1
```

#### OR Query

Find items matching any of multiple tracking codes:

```http
GET /v2/api/entity/purchase_order_item/list/1?filter[or][tracking_data][like]=LOT001&filter[or][tracking_data][like]=LOT002&filter[or][tracking_data][like]=LOT003
```

items.name: Overview
items.content:
# Multi-Entity Data Fetching (API)

## Overview

This feature lets you fetch data from **multiple entities in a single request** — whether you need a single record or a full list. Think of it as a backend-handled `Promise.all`: instead of firing several sequential API calls, you send one request and get all the results back together.

The result is fewer round trips and better performance on pages that need data from several entities at once.


---

## Endpoint

```
POST /v2/api/multi-entity
```

---

## Request Body

The body is an **array of entity requests**. Each request object supports the following fields:

| Field | Required | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `key` | Yes | The entity to fetch (e.g. `invoice`, `requisition`, `le_187`). |
| `action` | Yes | `"show"` to fetch a single record, or `"list"` to fetch a collection. |
| `options` | Yes | For `show`: the record **ID**. For `list`: filter **key-value pairs**. |
| `data_level` | Yes | Controls the depth of data returned: `-1`, `1`, `2`, `3`, or `4` — the same data level previously appended to endpoint URLs. |
| `alias` | Optional | A custom name for the response key of the fetched entity. Recommended when fetching the same entity more than once, to avoid key conflicts. |

> **Note on filters:** for `list` actions, filters are passed as `key: value` directly — **not** in the `filter[key]=value` format.

---

## Example Request

```json
[
  {
    "key": "invoice",
    "action": "show",
    "options": "172185",
    "data_level": 1
  },
  {
    "key": "requisition",
    "action": "list",
    "options": { "order_id": "172185", "order_type": 3 },
    "alias": "requisition"
  }
]
```

---

## Example Response

```json
{
  "invoice": {
    "id": 172185,
    "no": "*64500",
    "date": "2025-10-29",
    "client_id": 52739,
    "payment_status": 2,
    "summary_total": 9978,
    "summary_paid": 9978,
    "summary_unpaid": 0,
    "staff": { ... },
    "clients": { ... },
    "invoice_item": [ ... ],
    "payment": [ ... ]
  },
  "requisition": {
    "current_page": 1,
    "data": [
      {
        "id": 206298,
        "number": "145749",
        "status": 3,
        "type": 2,
        "order_id": 172185,
        "order_number": "*64500",
        "date": "2025-11-03 17:35:00"
      }
    ],
    "total": 1,
    "per_page": 20
  }
}
```

---

## Response Shape by Action

The structure returned depends on the `action`:

| Action | Returns |
| --- | --- |
| `show` | A **single record object** directly under the entity key. |
| `list` | A **paginated object** containing `data`, `total`, `current_page`, `per_page`, and similar fields. |

Each entity's result is keyed by its `key` — or by its `alias`, if one was provided. Using an `alias` is the way to avoid collisions when the same entity is fetched more than once in a single request.

---

## Why Use It

By batching several fetches into one call, this endpoint **reduces API round trips** and **improves performance** for any page that needs data from multiple entities simultaneously. 🚀

items.name: Introduction & Getting Started
items.content:
# Introduction & Getting Started

## What is the Developer Portal?

The **Daftra Developer Portal** is where developers and partners build, configure, publish, and earn from custom apps that extend Daftra. From a single dashboard you can create an app, define its data and behavior, control who can install it, publish it to the Daftra marketplace, and track the earnings it generates.

This guide walks through the portal one section at a time. By the end you'll be able to take an app from a first idea all the way to a published product.

---

## Creating Your Developer Account

To use the portal, you first need a developer account via this link: [Developer Portal](https://developers.daftra.com/login)


![Daftra-Marketplace-06-21-2026_02_20_PM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378309/image-preview)

On the sign-in screen you'll find:

- **Email** and **Password** fields to log in to an existing account.
- **Forgot Password?** to reset your credentials.
- **No account? Sign Up** — use this link the first time to register a new developer account.

**To get started:**

1. Open the Developer Portal.
2. If you don't have an account yet, click **Sign Up** and complete registration.
3. Once registered, return to this screen, enter your **Email** and **Password**, and click **Sign In**.

After signing in, you land on your portal dashboard.

---

## Navigating the Portal

The portal uses a simple left-hand sidebar that stays visible as you work. It has three main areas:

| Menu item               | What it's for                                                                           |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Dashboard**           | Your earnings overview — balances and transaction history.                              |
| **My Apps**             | The list of every app you've created, and the entry point for editing or managing them. |
| **Withdrawal Requests** | Where you request payouts of your available earnings.                                   |

At the bottom of the sidebar you'll see your **account profile** (name and email) and a **Sign Out** option.

You'll also find the **Add New App** button — this is how you start building a brand-new app.


![Daftra-Partners-06-22-2026_01_15_PM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378324/image-preview)

---

## The Journey at a Glance

Building and publishing an app in the portal follows a clear path. Each step has its own page later in this guide:

1. **Create the app** — give it a name, type, icon, and description.
2. **Configure it** — set its redirect URI, categories, permissions, and availability.
3. **Manage it** — work from the app's dashboard, where its credentials and tools live.
4. **Add data & behavior** — build entities and fields, then extend the interface with buttons, page scripts, and webhooks.
5. **Distribute it** — invite specific accounts privately, or publish to the marketplace.
6. **Earn** — track balances and request withdrawals as your app generates revenue.

items.name: Dashboard, Earnings & Withdrawals
items.content:
# Dashboard, Earnings & Withdrawals

The **Dashboard** is the first screen you see after signing in. It gives you a clear picture of how much your apps have earned, what's available to withdraw, and a full history of your transactions.


![Daftra-Partners-06-21-2026_02_21_PM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378311/image-preview)

---

## Your Balances

At the top of the dashboard are three balance cards. Together they show the full state of your earnings:

| Balance               | Meaning                                                            |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Total Balance**     | Everything you've earned — held and available combined.            |
| **Held Balance**      | Earnings that are still on hold and not yet available to withdraw. |
| **Available Balance** | Earnings that have cleared and are ready to withdraw.              |

In short: **Total Balance = Held Balance + Available Balance.** As held earnings clear, they move out of _Held_ and into _Available_.

---

## The Transactions List

Below the balances is a **Transactions** table showing the individual earnings your apps have generated. Each row includes:

| Column               | Description                                                         |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Description**      | What the transaction is for (for example, an app subscription fee). |
| **Transaction Date** | When the transaction was recorded.                                  |
| **Amount**           | The value of the transaction.                                       |
| **Status**           | Whether the amount is **On Hold** or **Available**.                 |

Transactions appear here automatically as your published apps are subscribed to and used — for example, recurring **App Subscription Fee** entries from clients who installed your app.

---

## Held vs. Available — Why Earnings Are On Hold

When an app generates revenue, the earnings don't become withdrawable instantly. Each transaction first appears with an **On Hold** status and is counted in your **Held Balance**. Once it clears, its status changes to **Available** and the amount moves into your **Available Balance**, ready to withdraw.

This is normal and applies to every earning. The dashboard always reflects the current split, so you can see at a glance what's cleared and what's still pending.

---

## Requesting a Withdrawal

When you have funds in your **Available Balance**, you can request a payout from the **Withdrawal Requests** section in the left sidebar.



![Daftra-Partners-06-22-2026_11_25_AM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378319/image-preview)


From here you submit a request to withdraw your available earnings. Only funds that have cleared (status **Available**) can be withdrawn

items.name: Creating a New App
items.content:
# Creating a New App

To build a new app, click **Add New App** from the sidebar. This opens the **Create New App** form, which starts with the app's **Basic Details** — its core identity. Everything here can be edited later, but it's worth getting right since it shapes how clients see your app.


![Daftra-Partners-06-22-2026_12_57_PM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378320/image-preview)

---

## Basic Details

### App Name (English & Arabic)

- **App Name (English)** — _required._ The name shown to English-language accounts.
- **اسم التطبيق (اللغة العربية)** — the Arabic name, shown to Arabic-language accounts.

Daftra displays the right version automatically based on each client's account language, so treat both as complete, polished names rather than rough translations.

### Type

Choose the kind of app you're building — _required._ The available types are:

| Type         | Use it for                                          |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------------- |
| **General**  | A standard app that adds features or functionality. |
| **Template** | An app that provides a template.                    |
| **Report**   | An app that provides reporting.                     |

### Icon

Upload an icon to represent your app in listings. Use the **Browse** button to select a file.

- Accepted formats: **JPEG, PNG, or JPG**.
- Keep the file small — the maximum allowed size is **1 MB**.

A clear, recognizable icon helps clients spot your app quickly.

### Description (English & Arabic)

- **Description (English)** — _required._ Explains what your app does, shown to English-language accounts.
- **الوصف (اللغة العربية)** — the Arabic description, shown to Arabic-language accounts.

This is your app's main pitch. Describe clearly what it does and the value it provides. As with the name, write each language as a full description on its own.

### Contact Details

- **Contact Mail** — a support email where clients can reach you.
- **Contact Phone** — a support phone number.

These appear to clients as your app's official support channel, so keep them accurate.

---

## Saving the App

When you've filled in the details, use **Save** (top right) to create the app, or **Cancel** to discard. After saving, the app is created and you can move on to configuring it.

> 💡 You can return to all of these fields at any time through **Edit App → Basic Details**.

items.name: App Configuration
items.content:
# App Configuration

After the basics, the **App Config** tab controls how your app connects, who can find it, what it's allowed to do, and where it's available. This page also covers the **Media Upload** tab for adding screenshots to your app's listing.


![Daftra-Partners-06-22-2026_12_59_PM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378321/image-preview)

---

## App Config

### Valid OAuth Redirect URI

- _Required._ The URL Daftra returns users to after they log in through your app.
- A manually specified `redirect_uri` used with Login on the web **must exactly match** this URI.
- It must be a full `https://` address.

Getting this exactly right is important — even a small mismatch will break the login flow.

### Categories

- _Required._ One or more categories that describe what your app relates to. Categories help clients discover your app in the marketplace.

Available categories include: Accounting, Discuss, Document Management, eCommerce, Human Resources, Industries, Localization, Manufacturing, Marketing, Point of Sale, Productivity, Project, Purchases, Sales, Warehouse, Website, Extra Tools, and Tutorial.

### Permissions

- _Required._ Controls what your app is allowed to access in a client's account. There are two modes:

| Mode                   | What it does                                                 |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Full Permissions**   | Grants the app broad access across the account.              |
| **Custom Permissions** | Lets you grant only the specific permissions your app needs. |

When you choose **Custom Permissions**, a **Custom Permissions** selector appears where you pick exactly what the app can do. Permissions come in **View** (read) and **Manage** (create/update/delete) pairs for each resource — for example _View Invoices_ / _Manage Invoices_, _View Clients_ / _Manage Clients_, _View Products_ / _Manage Products_, and so on across invoices, estimates, credit notes, refund receipts, work orders, appointments, payments, journals, expenses, taxes, purchase invoices, stock, stores, treasuries, and more. There's also an **All Permissions** option.

> 💡 Wherever possible, prefer **Custom Permissions** scoped to exactly what your app needs, rather than requesting Full access.

### Countries

- Optional. Restrict where your app can be installed. Leave it set to **All Countries** to allow everywhere, or select specific countries to limit availability.

### Industries

- Optional. Restrict your app to specific industries. Leave it set to **All Industries** to allow all, or choose the industries your app is built for.

---

## Media Upload

The **Media Upload** tab lets you add images that showcase your app on its listing.

- Click the upload area to select files, or drag and drop them in.
- Accepted formats: **PNG, JPG, JPEG**.
- Maximum size: **2 MB each**.
- You can upload multiple images at once and clear or remove them before saving.

---

## Saving Your Configuration

Use **Save** (top right) to apply your changes. As with Basic Details, everything here remains editable later through **Edit App → App Config**.

items.name: Managing Your App
items.content:
#  Managing Your App

Once an app is created, it gets its own page — the **App Hub**. This is the control center for everything about your app: its status, its credentials, its earnings at a glance, and the tools for building out its features. You reach it by opening an app from **My Apps**.


---

## The Header: Status & Actions

At the top of the App Hub you'll see the app's **name**, its **categories**, and a status badge such as **Sandbox** (an app stays in Sandbox until it's published).

Alongside are the main action buttons:

| Action | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| **Request Publish** | Submits the app for review so it can be published to the marketplace. |
| **Edit App** | Opens the Basic Details / App Config / Media Upload form (Pages 3–4). |
| **App Settings** | Opens advanced settings for the app. |
| **▾ (dropdown)** | Reveals additional actions for the app. |

---

## The Tabs

Below the header is a row of tabs that organize everything your app contains:

| Tab | What's inside |
| --- | --- |
| **Info** | Overview of the app — insights, pricing, and credentials (this page). |
| **Reviews** | Reviews left by users after the app is published. |
| **Entities** | Custom data structures your app defines (Pages 6–7). |
| **Buttons** | Custom buttons added into the Daftra interface. |
| **Page Scripts** | Scripts that run on specific pages. |
| **Webhooks** | Event notifications sent to your servers. |

---

## The Info Tab

The **Info** tab gives you a complete snapshot of the app, divided into three sections.

### Insights

Live metrics about how your app is performing:

| Metric | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| **App Rating** | The app's average user rating. |
| **Current Earnings** | What the app has earned for you. |
| **Current Revenue** | The revenue the app has generated. |
| **Total Installation Count** | How many accounts have installed the app. |
| **Current Active Users** | How many accounts are actively using it. |
| **Client ID** | The app's unique identifier (used for integration). |

### Pricing

Your app's commercial setup — **Price** (for example, *Free*), **Extra Fees Per User**, **Payment Plan**, and **Trial Period**.

### Data

The app's key configuration and credentials:

| Field | Notes |
| --- | --- |
| **Requested Permissions** | The permission level the app requests (e.g. *All Permissions*). |
| **Description** | The app's description. |
| **Client Secret** | A secret key used for authentication — hidden by default, with show/hide and **Copy** controls. Keep it private. |
| **Redirect** | The app's OAuth redirect URI, with a **Copy** control. |
| **Contact Email / Contact Phone** | Your support details. |

items.name: Entities: Modeling Your Data
items.content:
#  Entities: Modeling Your Data

**Entities** are the custom data structures your app uses. An entity might be a brand-new kind of record your app owns, an extra set of fields added onto an existing Daftra record, or a related form attached to one. You create and manage entities from the **Entities** tab on the App Hub.

To add one, open the **Entities** tab and choose to create a new entity — this opens the **Create Entity** form.


---

## Create Entity

The form has a few fields:

### Entity Title

*Required.* The name of your entity (for example, *Routes* or *Visits*).

### Entity Description

An optional description explaining what the entity is for.

### Entity Type

This is the key choice — it defines how your entity relates to the rest of Daftra. There are four options:

| Entity Type | What it does | Use it when… |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Main Entity** | A standalone data structure your app owns, with no direct link to existing records. | You need a brand-new kind of data of its own. |
| **Extend Global Entity** | Adds extra custom fields onto an existing Daftra record. | You want to attach extra data to a Client, Employee, Product, or Supplier. |
| **Related Entity** | A related form/record tied to an existing record. | You need supplementary forms attached to an existing record. |
| **Workflow** | Relates multiple forms/entities together into a connected process. | Your app's logic spans more than one entity. |

### Related Entity (Base Entity)

When you choose a type that connects to existing data (**Extend Global Entity** or **Related Entity**), a second dropdown appears so you can pick which existing record it attaches to:

- **Client**
- **Employee**
- **Product**
- **Supplier**

For example, choosing *Extend Global Entity* + *Client* adds your custom fields to every Client record; choosing *Related Entity* + *Client* attaches a related form to Clients.

---

## Saving the Entity

Use **Save** (top right) to create the entity, or **Cancel** to discard. Once saved, the entity appears under the **Entities** tab, where you can open it to build out its fields — covered on the next page.

> 💡 Choosing the right entity type up front matters, because it determines whether your data stands alone, extends an existing record, or links several together.

items.name: The Entity Builder
items.content:
# The Entity Builder: Fields, Validation & Layout

Once an entity is created, you define what it actually stores using the **Entity Builder**. The builder has three areas: a **field palette** on one side (the field types you can add), the **form canvas** in the middle (your entity as it's being built), and a **properties panel** on the other side (settings for the selected field).

You add a field by choosing it from the palette, then configure it in the properties panel.


> 💡 The builder is designed for desktop. Daftra recommends using a PC for entity field setup rather than a mobile device.

---

## The Field Palette

The builder offers a wide range of field types. They group naturally by purpose:

**Text fields**

- **Single Line Field** — short text on one line.
- **Multi-Lines Field** — longer, multi-line text.
- **URL Field** — a web address.
- **Email Field** — an email address.
- **Phone Number** — a phone number.

**Dates & numbers**

- **Date** — a calendar date.
- **Time** — a time value.
- **Currency** — a monetary amount.
- **Number** — a numeric value.

**Choices & toggles**

- **Toggle** — an on/off switch.
- **Dropdown** — pick one option from a list.
- **Multiple Dropdown** — pick several options.
- **Dynamic Dropdown** — pick one option from a data-driven list.
- **Multiple Dynamic Dropdown** — pick several from a data-driven list.

**Media & location**

- **Map Location** — a location on a map.
- **File** — a file upload.
- **Image** — an image upload.

**Structure & display**

- **Multiple Sub-Forms** — a repeatable group of fields.
- **Separator** — a visual divider between sections.
- **Static Content** — fixed content shown on the form.
- **Formatted Text** — rich/formatted text.

---

## Field Properties

When you select a field, the **Properties** panel lets you configure it:

| Property | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| **Field Label** | The label shown to the user for this field. |
| **Key** | The field's unique internal identifier. Max **15 characters**, using only letters, numbers, and underscores. |
| **Default Value** | A value pre-filled when a new record is created. |
| **Placeholder** | Hint text shown inside the empty field. |
| **Filter by this Field?** | Allows records to be filtered by this field in the listing. |
| **List by this Field?** | Shows this field as a column in the entity's listing. |
| **Auto Suggest** | Suggests previously entered values as the user types. |

---

## Validations

Control what counts as valid input:

| Validation | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| **Min Length** | The minimum number of characters allowed. |
| **Max Length** | The maximum number of characters allowed. |
| **Is Unique?** | Requires the value to be unique — no two records can share it. |
| **Is Required?** | Makes the field mandatory; the record can't be saved without it. |

---

## Layout

Control how the field appears on the form:

| Option | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| **Allow Beside** | Lets the field sit beside another field on the same line instead of on its own row. |
| **Field Size** | Sets the field's width (for example, **Small, X-Small, Medium, Large, X-Large**). |

---

## Saving the Entity

Give the entity its label at the top of the builder, then use **Save** to store it (or **Cancel** to discard). After saving, you can reopen the builder anytime to **edit** the fields or **delete** the entity.

items.name: Buttons & Page Scripts
items.content:
# Buttons & Page Scripts

Beyond data, your app can extend the Daftra **interface** so it feels built-in. Two tools make this possible: custom **Buttons** that appear inside Daftra pages, and **Page Scripts** that run custom logic on specific pages. Both live as tabs on the App Hub.

---

## Buttons

The **Buttons** tab lets you add your own buttons into the Daftra interface, so users can trigger your app's actions right where they need them.


When creating a button you typically set:

| Setting | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| **Icon** | A recognizable icon for the button. |
| **Location** | Where the button appears in Daftra (for example, on the Invoice index page). |
| **Active / Not Active** | Whether the button is currently shown. |
| **Order** | The button's position relative to others. |
| **Parameters / Attributes** | Additional values the button passes or uses. |

Together, **location** and **active state** decide exactly where — and whether — the button shows up.

---

## Page Scripts

The **Page Scripts** tab lets you run custom code on specific Daftra pages. To add one, create a new script — this opens the **Create Script** form.


The form has these fields:

| Field | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| **Name** | A name to identify the script. |
| **Depend on** | An optional plugin the script depends on (*Select a Plugin*). |
| **URL** | The page path where the script should run (for example, `/owner/invoices/view`). |
| **Match URL exactly** | When on, the script runs only when the page URL matches the value exactly — not just any page that starts with it. |
| **Active Script** | When on, the script is live and will execute. When off, it's saved but won't run. |
| **Script** | The code editor where you write the script itself (wrapped in `<script>` tags). |

**How it works:** the script runs automatically on pages matching the **URL** you set. Use **Match URL exactly** to limit it to one precise page, and the **Active Script** toggle to turn it on or off without deleting it.

---

## Saving

Use **Save** (top right) on either form to apply your changes, or **Cancel** to discard.

> 💡 Keep scripts inactive while you're still testing — toggle **Active Script** on only when you're ready for it to run on real pages.

items.name: Integrations: Webhooks
items.content:
# Integrations: Webhooks

**Webhooks** connect your app to the outside world. Instead of constantly asking Daftra whether something changed, a webhook lets Daftra **notify your server the moment an event happens** — for example, when an invoice is created or a client is updated. You manage them from the **Webhooks** tab on the App Hub.

To add one, open the **Webhooks** tab and create a new webhook — this opens the **Create Webhook** form.


![Daftra-Partners-06-22-2026_01_02_PM.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/378323/image-preview)
---

## Create Webhook

The form has two fields:

### URL

The endpoint on your server that receives the notification. When the chosen event fires, Daftra sends the data to this address.

- *Required.*
- Must start with **`https://`**.

### Event

The event that triggers the webhook. Pick one from the dropdown. The available events cover several resources, with create / update / delete actions:

| Resource | Available events |
| --- | --- |
| **Invoice** | Invoice created, Invoice deleted |
| **Client** | Client created, Client updated, Client deleted |
| **Supplier** | Supplier created, Supplier updated, Supplier deleted |
| **Product** | Product created, Product updated, Product deleted |
| **Item Group** | Item Group created, Item Group deleted |
| **Tax** | Tax deleted |
| **Refund Receipt** | Refund Receipt deleted |
| **Account** | Reset Account |
| **App** | App Status Change |

When the selected event occurs in a client's account, Daftra sends a notification to your URL so your system can react in real time.

---

## Saving

Use **Save** (top right) to create the webhook, or **Cancel** to discard. Once saved, the webhook is active and will fire whenever its event occurs.

> 💡 Make sure your endpoint is ready to receive and acknowledge requests before going live — and use an `https://` URL, as required.

items.name: Invitations, Publishing & Reviews
items.content:
#  Distribution: Invitations, Publishing & Reviews

Once your app is built and configured, the last step is getting it into users' hands. The portal offers two paths: **Invitations** for sharing privately with specific accounts, and **Publishing** for releasing publicly to the marketplace. After publishing, users can leave **Reviews**.

---

## Invitations — Private Sharing

Invitations let you activate your app for **specific accounts without publishing it** — ideal for a particular client, internal use, or testing before a public launch. You reach this from the app's **Invitations** screen.


**To invite an account:**

1. Enter the account's email in the **E-mail** field.
2. Click **Invite**.

The invited account can then activate your app directly, without it appearing in the public marketplace. Each invitation you send is tracked in the list below the field, so you can see who's been invited and the status of each. (Before you've sent any, the screen simply shows that there are no invitations yet.)

Use **Back To App** to return to the App Hub.

---

## Publishing — Public Release

To make your app available to **all** Daftra users, publish it to the marketplace. Because a published app is visible to everyone, it goes through a review first.

**The flow:**

1. From the App Hub, click **Request Publish**.
2. Your app enters a **review** process.
3. Once approved, the app is **published** and appears in the marketplace for eligible users to discover and install.

Until it's published, your app stays in **Sandbox** status (shown on the App Hub).

---

## Reviews — User Feedback

After your app is published, users can rate and review it. These reviews appear under the **Reviews** tab on the App Hub.


Reviews build your app's reputation in the marketplace and give you feedback to improve it over time. The app's overall **App Rating** (shown in the Info tab's Insights) reflects this feedback.

---

## Choosing Your Path

- **Building for a specific client or small group?** Use **Invitations** and invite them by email.
- **Want the app discoverable by all Daftra users?** Use **Request Publish** and pass review.
- **Testing before launch?** Invite a few accounts first, then publish once you're confident.

items.name: Welcome
items.content:
This is the official developer portal for Daftra’s RESTful API — a powerful interface to connect with Daftra’s cloud-based business management platform. Whether you're creating new applications, integrating existing systems, or automating internal workflows, Daftra provides flexible and secure tools to extend your capabilities.

Through this API, you can programmatically interact with essential business modules such as:

- Invoicing and Billing  
- Client Management (CRM)  
- Inventory and Products  
- Accounting and Expenses  
- Human Resources  
- Reporting and more  

---

## Who Should Use This Documentation?

This documentation is designed for:

- Backend developers looking to integrate Daftra into custom systems  
- Technology partners building SaaS tools or platform extensions  
- Businesses automating workflows or syncing data across systems (ERP, e-commerce, etc.)  

---

## Key Features of the API

- **RESTful and JSON-based**: Our API is RESTful and JSON-based, using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) with predictable JSON response structures throughout. 
- **Secure Authentication**: Support for API Key and token-based OAuth2 methods  
- **Modular Architecture**: Endpoints are grouped logically by modules (Invoices, Clients, Products, etc.)  


---

## What You’ll Learn in This Documentation

- **Authentication Methods**  
  How to use Daftra API Key or request access tokens for secure connections  

- **Endpoint Reference**  
  A complete list of available API endpoints grouped by module with all required parameters and expected responses  

- **Use Case Examples**  
  Implementation examples with clear request/response formats and practical context  

- **Code Snippets**  
  Reusable code samples available in cURL, Python, Node.js, PHP, and more  

- **Error Codes and Handling**  
  Understand and manage common response codes including authentication, validation, and server-side errors  

- **Performance Best Practices**  
  Optimize API usage through pagination, batch processing, and rate handling techniques  

---

## Documentation Structure

| Section              | Description |
|----------------------|-------------|
| **Authentication**   | How to authenticate using API Key or token-based method |
| **API Endpoints**    | Explore endpoints grouped by modules such as Clients, Invoices, Products | |
| **Error Handling**   | Learn about error formats and how to resolve issues efficiently |
| **Code Samples**     | Ready-to-use examples in multiple programming languages |
| **Rate Limits**      | Understand daily request limits, throttling, and performance guidelines |

items.name: Getting Started
items.content:
Welcome to Daftra's developer environment! This guide is designed to help you take the first steps in working with Daftra's RESTful API.

Before jumping into implementation, it's important to understand how the Daftra API works, what's required to begin, and where to find the tools you'll need.

---

## Prerequisites

Before you can make your first API request, ensure the following:
- You have an active **Daftra account**
- You have the appropriate access permissions to manage API credentials
- You understand which Daftra modules (e.g., invoicing, CRM, inventory) your integration will use

---

## Initial Setup

Here's a basic roadmap to get started:

1. **Review the API Structure**  
   Familiarize yourself with Daftra's available modules and supported endpoints.

2. **Choose Your Authentication Method**  
   Learn how to authenticate using either an API key or a token-based approach in the [Authorization](./933385m0) section.

3. **Explore the API Endpoints**  
   Visit the [Endpoints](./15115241e0) and select the modules you want to integrate with.

4. **Test in an Environment**  
   Test your API requests using tools like **Postman** or Daftra's interactive testing environment

---

## Ready to Integrate?

Once you've reviewed this guide, move on to the [Authorization](./authorization.md) page to set up your API access and start sending requests.

items.name: Validation Errors
items.content:
Some requests may fail due to validation-related issues, such as missing or improperly formatted data already stored in the Daftra system. This guide outlines frequent validation errors and how to resolve them.

## 1. Invoicing Method Issues

**Error:** Sending invoice via email without providing a client email.

When the option to send the invoice by email is selected, but no email address is provided for the client, the system will reject the request with a validation error.

**Error Response Example:**

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358305/image-preview)

### How to Fix:
There are two ways to fix the previous error.

#### **The First Way:**
Make sure to enter a valid client email address by the following steps:
1- From the main menu, click on **"Clients"** 
2- Press on **"Manage Clients"**

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358315/image-preview)

3- Click on the **"Action Botton"** besides the client you want to add the email to.
4- Press on **"Edit"**

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358318/image-preview)

4- Enter the client's email in the **"Email"** field.

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358319/image-preview)

5- Click on **"Save"** Botton. 

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358323/image-preview)

#### ****The Secod Way:****

1- From the main menu, Click on **"Sales"**
2- Press on **"Sales Settings"**

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358324/image-preview)

3- Click on **"General iinvoice/Estimate Settings"**

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358325/image-preview)

4- From the **"Invoicing Method"** dropdown menu, choose **"Electronically via Email"**


![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358328/image-preview)

5- Press on **"Save"**

![image.png](https://api.apidog.com/api/v1/projects/846139/resources/358330/image-preview)

responseExamples.name: Example 1
Example 1

staff_id.description: Staff user working on this time tracking get it from [GET STAFF API](/15115376e0) if it's `0` that means the site owner is the staff

project_id.description: Project REF#, you can get it through the get it from [GET General Listing API with model `Product`](/15115384e0)

activity_id.description: Activity REF#, you can get it through the get it from [GET General Listing API with model `TimeActivity`](/15115384e0)

date.description: Date for the time to be tracked if empty the current date is used

notes.description: Notes for the time tracking entry

created.description: Time of creation for the Client
